text.title: DDD Aggregates Core Rules
text.summary: Five testable rules grounding the Aggregate Root pattern.

rule.statement: The Aggregate Root MUST be the only entry point for mutation. External code MUST NOT reach into child Entities or Value Objects and mutate them. All state changes go through named command methods on the root (`order.add_item(...)`, never `order.items.append(...)`).
rule.rationale: Without single-root entry, invariants spanning the aggregate cannot be enforced consistently; callers can leave the aggregate in an inconsistent state.

rule.statement: Each command method that changes aggregate state MUST raise a Domain Event (appended to an internal `_events` list). The application layer collects events via `aggregate.collect_events()` only after the DB transaction commits.
rule.rationale: Events make state changes observable to other bounded contexts without coupling. Collection-after-commit prevents publishing events for transactions that roll back.

rule.statement: Aggregates MUST NOT hold direct object references to other Aggregate Roots. Reference by identity (UUID, opaque ID) only. Cross-aggregate access goes through Repositories or Domain Services in the application layer.
rule.rationale: Object refs across aggregates blur transaction boundaries, force eager loading, and create cycles that break repository design.

rule.statement: Each invariant MUST exist as a failing test before the implementation. Test names match the invariant: `test_cannot_place_empty_order`, `test_cannot_ship_unpaid_order`, `test_cannot_modify_placed_order`.
rule.rationale: Invariants written only as code drift with refactors; expressed as tests they become regression gates.

rule.statement: An Aggregate SHOULD encompass at most ~5 child entities and load in a single DB transaction. If a candidate aggregate grows beyond this, split it along a sub-invariant boundary.
rule.rationale: Large aggregates cause lock contention, slow load, and weak invariant boundaries; small aggregates are the empirical sweet spot.
